title: Catechist's Guide — The Desire for God
html:
Catechist's Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Lesson summary

Estimated duration: 50-60 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: The desire for God is written in the human heart. It is not something we learn, it is something we are.

Objectives:

  • That the student understands that the heart's restlessness is a sign of our vocation to God.
  • That they identify the obstacles that prevent them from recognizing that desire.
  • That they discover that God seeks us first.

Key verse: St. Augustine, Confessions I,1 («You have made us for yourself, O Lord»)

Catechism references: CCC 27-30, 44-45

Suggested class structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Welcome and ice-breaker 5 min Greet, create atmosphere. Question: «What's the best thing that happened to you this week?» Listen.
2. Fulton Sheen's story 8 min Tell the anecdote of the woman and the God-shaped hole in your own words. Ask: «Do you identify?»
3. Main content 20 min Present the 4 sections of content. Read CCC 27 together. Explain the 7 obstacles. Use Psalm 42.
4. Group activity 10 min Choose a workbook activity according to age and group. For children: draw the heart. For youth: write their version of Psalm 42.
5. Sharing 8 min Students share what they discovered (optional, don't force).
6. Final prayer and closing 4 min Pray together the class prayer. Remember St. Augustine's verse.

Notes for the catechist

About Fulton Sheen

Venerable Fulton J. Sheen (1895-1979) was bishop of Rochester and a pioneer of television catechesis. His program «Life Is Worth Living» reached 30 million weekly viewers in the 1950s, surpassing secular programs like those of Frank Sinatra or Milton Berle. He was a converter of souls: among his famous converts are the communist Louis Budenz, writer Clare Boothe Luce, and politician Heywood Broun. Sheen had a special gift for making the deepest truths accessible through everyday stories.

For deeper study: It is recommended to read Sheen's Peace of Soul (1949) and Way to Happiness (1953). Both are available in affordable editions and online Catholic libraries.

About reflective questions

The workbook questions are optional. Not all groups are ready for the same depth. As a catechist, trust your intuition: if you see the group is receptive, go deeper. If you see resistance or discomfort, give space and return to basics: God's love that seeks us first.

Age adaptation

Children (7-12): Use the thirst metaphor. Bring a glass of water and ask: «What happens if I'm very thirsty and only drink a little?» The heart activity is ideal for them. They can use colors.

Youth (13-17): The existential approach works better. Ask: «Why after an incredible concert or party, do you sometimes feel empty?» Connect with the «high and low» experience they know well. The testimony of Carlo Acutis (the young saint of technology, canonized in 2025) is very powerful for them: he had everything a young person could desire, but only God filled him.

New adults: Skepticism is normal and healthy. Don't fight it, welcome it. An adult who asks is seeking. Use the thirst argument: «Thirst doesn't prove water exists, but it makes it probable.» The testimony of converted agnostics (C.S. Lewis, Chesterton) is very useful here.

Formed adults: Don't skip the lesson even if it's «basic». The depth is in the nuances: St. Thomas's desiderium naturale, the distinction between natural capacity and gift of grace, the relationship between desire for God and Incarnation.

Complementary resources

  • YouCat 1-4: The Youth Catechism opens exactly with this question. Excellent for adolescents.
  • Opus Dei, «The Longing for God»: Theme 1 of the series «Themes of the Christian Faith» at opusdei.org. Brief, clear, profound.
  • Infinito + 1: Series «Catequizis» by Juan Manuel Cotelo on YouTube. Short and pleasant videos.
  • Real + True (OSV): Episode «Made for More» about CCC 27-30. Available at osvnews.com.
  • Fr. Luis Toro: Catechesis on YouTube with clear language for youth and adults.

Possible difficult questions (and how to answer them)

«I don't feel that desire for God»

Don't worry. Sometimes the desire is there but we call it by another name: nostalgia, restlessness, desire for «something more». Think of those moments when something beautiful overwhelms you, or when you feel life should have more meaning. That is the desire for God. Not everyone recognizes it at first.

«If God exists, why does He allow suffering?»

It's the most serious question of all. It doesn't have an easy answer, and no one expects you to have it in five minutes. You can respond: «That question is a sign that your heart seeks justice and meaning, and that itself is a reflection of God. I don't have an answer that erases pain, but I have a certainty: God is not indifferent. On the cross, He Himself bore suffering.» Don't try to resolve the mystery. Accompany it.

«Religion is just a psychological crutch»

If religion were only a human projection, it wouldn't explain why it generates saints — people who give their lives for others, who forgive the unforgivable, who live with a joy that nothing and no one can take away. Crutches are used to support yourself; faith, on the other hand, puts you on the way.

«I don't need God to be good»

It's true: you can be a morally good person without explicit faith. God's grace acts in ways we don't always recognize. But the question is not whether you can be good, but whether you can be complete. Goodness without God is possible; fullness without Him is not.

Catechist's prayer

Lord, you who called your apostles and made them fishers of men,
fill me also with your Spirit.
Do not let my words be empty,
but that through me you may speak.
Grant me the grace not to hinder,
to know how to listen,
and to always point toward you.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
